24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
147	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
148	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
149	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
150	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
151	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
152	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
153	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
154	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
155	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
156	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
157	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
158	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
159	\end{proof}
160	
161	\section{Projective dimension}
162	\label{sec:ab_pd}
163	
164	\begin{definition}
165	
166	\leanok
167	  [Projective dimension]
168	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
169	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
170	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
171	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
172	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
173	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
174	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
175	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
176	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
177	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
178	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
179	  \[
180	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
181	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
182	  \]
183	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
184	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
185	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
186	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
187	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
188	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
189	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
190	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
191	\end{definition}
192	
193	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
194	\begin{itemize}
195	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
196	    iff \(M\) is free);
197	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
198	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
199	\end{itemize}
200	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
201	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
202	
203	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
204	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
205	
206	\begin{lemma}
207	
208	\leanok
209	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
210	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
211	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
212	  
213	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
214	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
216	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
217	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
218	  %  \begin{enumerate}
219	  %  \item
220	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
221	  %  \item
222	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
223	  %  \item
224	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
225	  %  \end{enumerate}"
226	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
227	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
228	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
229	  \(R\)-modules. Then
230	  \begin{enumerate}
231	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
232	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
233	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
234	  \end{enumerate}
235	\end{lemma}
236	
237	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
238	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
239	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
240	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
241	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
242	%  from Lemma REF."
243	\begin{proof}
244	  
245	  By Lemma~REF, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
246	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
247	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
248	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
249	  \[
250	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
251	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
252	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
253	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
254	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
255	  \]
256	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
257	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
258	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
259	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
260	\end{proof}
261	
262	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
263	\label{sec:ab_main}
264	
265	\begin{theorem}
266	
267	\leanok
268	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
269	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
270	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
271	  
272	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
273	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
274	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
275	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
276	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
277	  %  \(\)
278	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
279	  %  \(\)"
280	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
281	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
282	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
283	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
284	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
285	  \[
286	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
287	  \]
288	\end{theorem}
289	
290	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
291	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
292	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
293	%  \(\)
294	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
295	%  \(\)
296	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
297	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
298	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
299	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
300	%  Proposition REF we see that
301	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
302	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
303	%  \begin{align*}
304	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
305	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
306	%  \ldots,\\
307	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
308	%  \end{align*}
309	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
310	%  \begin{align*}
311	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
312	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
313	%  \ldots,\\
314	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
315	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
316	%  \end{align*}
317	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
318	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).
319	%
320	%  Induction step. If \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), then we pick \(x \in \mathfrak m\)
321	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both \(M\) and \(R\). This is possible, because
322	%  either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\) and \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the aforementioned
323	%  Proposition REF or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which
324	%  case \(M\) is finite free hence also \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\).
325	%  Thus \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by Lemma REF
326	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor
327	%  on both \(R\) and \(M\). Let
328	%  \(\)
329	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
330	%  \(\)
331	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
332	%  shows that
333	%  \(\)
334	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
335	%  M/xM \to 0
336	%  \(\)
337	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\).
338	%  By Lemma REF we have
339	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
340	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
341	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
342	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
343	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
344	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
345	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
346	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
347	\begin{proof}
348	  
349	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
350	
351	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
352	  finite free resolution
353	  \[
354	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
355	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
356	  \]
357	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
358	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
359	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
360	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
361	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
362	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
363	  sequences
364	  \[
365	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
366	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
367	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
368	  \]
369	  and applying Lemma~REF iteratively,
370	  yields the chain of inequalities
371	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
372	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
373	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
374	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
375	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
376	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
377	
378	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
379	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
380	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
381	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
382	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
383	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
384	  Lemma~REF, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
385	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
386	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
387	  \[
388	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
389	  \]
390	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
391	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
392	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
393	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
394	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
395	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
396	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
397	  \[
398	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
399	  \]
400	  Substituting yields
401	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
402	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
403	\end{proof}

405	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
406	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
407	
408	\begin{definition}
409	
410	\leanok
411	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
412	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
413	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
414	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
415	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
416	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
417	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
418	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
419	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
420	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
421	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
422	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
423	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
424	\end{definition}
425	
426	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly
427	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper
428	% `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
429	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
430	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the
431	% load-bearing sorry pushed into two named helpers: (i)
432	% `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` consolidating the
433	% Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gaps in one signature, and (ii) an inline
434	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
435	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
436	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
437	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker
438	% iter185-auslander minor flagged adding a pin for the public-facing
439	% `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` itself; iter-186+ chapter touch can add
440	% that hint). The Stacks 00NQ Mathlib gap remains the load-bearing substrate;
441	% iter-186+ work paths unchanged: (a) formalise Stacks 00NQ in project
442	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;
443	% (c) Mathlib upstream.
444	\begin{corollary}
445	
446	\leanok
447	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
448	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
449	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
450	  
451	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
452	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
453	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
454	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
455	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
456	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
457	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
458	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
459	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
460	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
461	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
462	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
463	\end{corollary}
464	
465	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
466	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
467	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
468	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
469	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
470	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
471	\begin{proof}
472	  
473	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
474	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
475	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
476	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
477	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
478	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
479	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
480	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
481	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
482	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
483	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
484	
485	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
486	  Theorem~REF as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
487	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
488	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
489	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
490	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
491	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
492	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
493	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
494	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
495	\end{proof}
496	
497	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
498	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
499	
500	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
501	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
502	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
503	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
504	
505	\begin{enumerate}
506	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
507	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
508	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
509	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
510	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
511	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
512	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
513	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
514	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
515	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
516	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
517	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
518	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
519	\end{enumerate}
520	
521	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
522	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
523	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
524	Corollary~REF are formalized in
525	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
526	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
527

594	    --- a downstream consequence of Stacks tag 0AVF; planned for its own chapter
595	    \texttt{Albanese\_LocalCohomology.tex} or as part of A.4.a directly.
596	  \item \textbf{Global Cohen--Macaulay schemes / global depth} --- this chapter only treats
597	    the local-ring case. The global-to-local reduction is standard but not packaged here.
598	  \item \textbf{General homological-dimension theory (global dimension, finitistic
599	    dimension)} --- only the local-ring projective-dimension invariant appears here.
600	  \item \textbf{Cohen--Macaulay characterisations via \(\omega_R\), dualising complex,
601	    Gorenstein refinement} --- the formula and its immediate Cohen--Macaulay corollary
602	    suffice for A.4.a; the duality side is not needed.
603	\end{itemize}

namespace Lean.Elab.Tactic

/-
  Given an `inductionAlt` of the form
  ```
  syntax inductionAltLHS := "| " (group("@"? ident) <|> hole) (ident <|> hole)*
  syntax inductionAlt  := ppDedent(ppLine) inductionAltLHS+ (" => " (hole <|> syntheticHole <|> tacticSeq))?
  ```
  We assume that the syntax has been expanded. There is exactly one `inductionAltLHS`,
  and `" => " (hole <|> syntheticHole <|> tacticSeq)` is present
-/
def getAltLhses (alt : Syntax) : Syntax :=
  alt[0]
def getFirstAltLhs (alt : Syntax) : Syntax :=
  (getAltLhses alt)[0]
/-- Return `inductionAlt` name. It assumes `alt` does not have multiple `inductionAltLHS`. Returns `none` if the name is missing. -/
def getAltName? (alt : Syntax) : Option Name :=
  let head := (getFirstAltLhs alt)[1]
  if head.isOfKind ``Parser.Term.hole then
    if head[0].isMissing then none else some `_
  else
    let ident := head[1]
    if ident.isOfKind identKind then some ident.getId.eraseMacroScopes else none

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.DFinsupp
9	public import Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Quotient
10	public import Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.RightExactness
11	
12	/-!
13	# Reducing a module modulo an element of the ring
14	
15	Given a commutative ring `R` and an element `r : R`, the association
16	`M ↦ M ⧸ rM` extends to a functor on the category of `R`-modules. This functor
17	is isomorphic to the functor of tensoring by `R ⧸ (r)` on either side, but can
18	be more convenient to work with since we can work with quotient types instead
19	of fiddling with simple tensors.
20	
21	## Tags
22	
23	module, commutative algebra
24	-/
25	
26	@[expose] public section
27	
28	open scoped Pointwise
29	
30	variable {R} [CommRing R] (r : R) (M : Type*) {M' M''}
31	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [AddCommGroup M'] [Module R M']
32	    [AddCommGroup M''] [Module R M'']
33	
34	/-- An abbreviation for `M⧸rM` that keeps us from having to write
35	`(⊤ : Submodule R M)` over and over to satisfy the typechecker. -/
36	abbrev QuotSMulTop := M ⧸ r • (⊤ : Submodule R M)
37	
38	namespace QuotSMulTop
39	
40	open Submodule Function TensorProduct
41	
42	/-- If `M'` is isomorphic to `M''` as `R`-modules, then `M'⧸rM'` is isomorphic to `M''⧸rM''`. -/
43	protected def congr (e : M' ≃ₗ[R] M'') : QuotSMulTop r M' ≃ₗ[R] QuotSMulTop r M'' :=
44	  Submodule.Quotient.equiv (r • ⊤) (r • ⊤) e <|
45	    (Submodule.map_pointwise_smul r _ e.toLinearMap).trans (by simp)
46	
47	/-- Reducing a module modulo `r` is the same as left tensoring with `R/(r)`. -/
48	noncomputable def equivQuotTensor :
49	    QuotSMulTop r M ≃ₗ[R] (R ⧸ Ideal.span {r}) ⊗[R] M :=
50	  quotEquivOfEq _ _ (ideal_span_singleton_smul _ _).symm ≪≫ₗ
51	    (quotTensorEquivQuotSMul M _).symm
52	
53	/-- Reducing a module modulo `r` is the same as right tensoring with `R/(r)`. -/
54	noncomputable def equivTensorQuot :
55	    QuotSMulTop r M ≃ₗ[R] M ⊗[R] (R ⧸ Ideal.span {r}) :=
56	  quotEquivOfEq _ _ (ideal_span_singleton_smul _ _).symm ≪≫ₗ
57	    (tensorQuotEquivQuotSMul M _).symm
58	
59	variable {M}
60	
61	/-- The action of the functor `QuotSMulTop r` on morphisms. -/
62	def map : (M →ₗ[R] M') →ₗ[R] QuotSMulTop r M →ₗ[R] QuotSMulTop r M' :=
63	  Submodule.mapQLinear _ _ ∘ₗ LinearMap.id.codRestrict _ fun _ =>
64	    map_le_iff_le_comap.mp <| le_of_eq_of_le (map_pointwise_smul _ _ _) <|

272

276	    IsWeaklyRegular M (r :: rs) :=

278	
279	/-- Weakly regular sequences can be inductively characterized by:
280	* The empty sequence is weakly regular on any module.
281	* If `r` is regular on `M` and `rs` is a weakly regular sequence on `M⧸rM` then
282	  the sequence obtained from `rs` by prepending `r` is weakly regular on `M`.
283	
284	This is the induction principle produced by the inductive definition above.
285	The motive will usually be valued in `Prop`, but `Sort*` works too. -/
286	@[induction_eliminator]
287	def recIterModByRegular
288	    {motive : (M : Type v) → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → (rs : List R) →
289	      IsWeaklyRegular M rs → Sort*}
290	    (nil : (M : Type v) → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → motive M [] (nil R M))
291	    (cons : {M : Type v} → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → (r : R) →
292	      (rs : List R) → (h1 : IsSMulRegular M r) →
293	      (h2 : IsWeaklyRegular (QuotSMulTop r M) rs) →
294	      (ih : motive (QuotSMulTop r M) rs h2) → motive M (r :: rs) (cons h1 h2)) :
295	    {M : Type v} → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → {rs : List R} →
296	    (h : IsWeaklyRegular M rs) → motive M rs h
297	  | M, _, _, [], _ => nil M
298	  | M, _, _, r :: rs, h =>
299	    let ⟨h1, h2⟩ := (isWeaklyRegular_cons_iff M r rs).mp h
300	    cons r rs h1 h2 (recIterModByRegular nil cons h2)
301	
302	/-- A simplified version of `IsWeaklyRegular.recIterModByRegular` where the
303	motive is not allowed to depend on the proof of `IsWeaklyRegular`. -/
304	def ndrecIterModByRegular
305	    {motive : (M : Type v) → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → (rs : List R) → Sort*}
306	    (nil : (M : Type v) → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → motive M [])
307	    (cons : {M : Type v} → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → (r : R) →
308	      (rs : List R) → IsSMulRegular M r → IsWeaklyRegular (QuotSMulTop r M) rs →
309	      motive (QuotSMulTop r M) rs → motive M (r :: rs))
310	    {M} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] {rs} :
311	    IsWeaklyRegular M rs → motive M rs :=
312	  recIterModByRegular (motive := fun M _ _ rs _ => motive M rs) nil cons
313	
314	/-- An alternate induction principle from `IsWeaklyRegular.recIterModByRegular`
315	where we mod out by successive elements in both the module and the base ring.
316	This is useful for propagating certain properties of the initial `M`, e.g.
317	faithfulness or freeness, throughout the induction. -/
318	def recIterModByRegularWithRing
319	    {motive : (R : Type u) → [CommRing R] → (M : Type v) → [AddCommGroup M] →
320	      [Module R M] → (rs : List R) → IsWeaklyRegular M rs → Sort*}
321	    (nil : (R : Type u) → [CommRing R] → (M : Type v) → [AddCommGroup M] →
322	      [Module R M] → motive R M [] (nil R M))
323	    (cons : {R : Type u} → [CommRing R] → {M : Type v} → [AddCommGroup M] →

Second, it tries to abstract these occurrences of 'a' to create a function 'm := fun _a => ...', called the *motive*, with the property that 'm a' is definitionally equal to 'e'. Third, we observe that 'congrArg' implies that 'm a = m b', which can be used with lemmas such as 'Eq.mpr' to change the goal. However, if 'e' depends on specific properties of 'a', then the motive 'm' might not typecheck.

Possible solutions: use rewrite's 'occs' configuration option to limit which occurrences are rewritten, or use 'simp' or 'conv' mode, which have strategies for certain kinds of dependencies (these tactics can handle proofs and 'Decidable' instances whose types depend on the rewritten term, and 'simp' can apply user-defined '@[congr]' theorems as well).

330:def Quotient.equiv {N : Type*} [AddCommGroup N] [Module R N] (P : Submodule R M)

325	  exact inf_le_right
326	
327	/-- If `P` is a submodule of `M` and `Q` a submodule of `N`,
328	and `f : M ≃ₗ N` maps `P` to `Q`, then `M ⧸ P` is equivalent to `N ⧸ Q`. -/
329	@[simps]
330	def Quotient.equiv {N : Type*} [AddCommGroup N] [Module R N] (P : Submodule R M)
331	    (Q : Submodule R N) (f : M ≃ₗ[R] N) (hf : P.map (f : M →ₗ[R] N) = Q) : (M ⧸ P) ≃ₗ[R] N ⧸ Q :=
332	  { P.mapQ Q (f : M →ₗ[R] N) fun _ hx => hf ▸ Submodule.mem_map_of_mem hx with
333	    toFun := P.mapQ Q (f : M →ₗ[R] N) fun _ hx => hf ▸ Submodule.mem_map_of_mem hx
334	    invFun :=
335	      Q.mapQ P (f.symm : N →ₗ[R] M) fun x hx => by
336	        rw [← hf, Submodule.mem_map] at hx
337	        obtain ⟨y, hy, rfl⟩ := hx
338	        simpa
339	    left_inv := fun x => Submodule.Quotient.induction_on _ x (by simp)
340	    right_inv := fun x => Submodule.Quotient.induction_on _ x (by simp) }
341	
342	@[simp]
343	theorem Quotient.equiv_symm {R M N : Type*} [Ring R] [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M]
344	    [AddCommGroup N] [Module R N] (P : Submodule R M) (Q : Submodule R N) (f : M ≃ₗ[R] N)
345	    (hf : P.map (f : M →ₗ[R] N) = Q) :
346	    (Quotient.equiv P Q f hf).symm =
347	      Quotient.equiv Q P f.symm ((Submodule.map_symm_eq_iff f).mpr hf) :=
348	  rfl
349	
350	@[simp]
351	theorem Quotient.equiv_trans {N O : Type*} [AddCommGroup N] [Module R N] [AddCommGroup O]
352	    [Module R O] (P : Submodule R M) (Q : Submodule R N) (S : Submodule R O) (e : M ≃ₗ[R] N)
353	    (f : N ≃ₗ[R] O) (he : P.map (e : M →ₗ[R] N) = Q) (hf : Q.map (f : N →ₗ[R] O) = S)
354	    (hef : P.map (e.trans f : M →ₗ[R] O) = S) :
355	    Quotient.equiv P S (e.trans f) hef =
356	      (Quotient.equiv P Q e he).trans (Quotient.equiv Q S f hf) := by
357	  ext
358	  -- `simp` can deal with `hef` depending on `e` and `f`
359	  simp only [Quotient.equiv_apply, LinearEquiv.trans_apply, LinearEquiv.coe_trans]
360	  -- `rw` can deal with `mapQ_comp` needing extra hypotheses coming from the RHS
361	  rw [mapQ_comp, LinearMap.comp_apply]
362	
363	end Submodule

# AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean

`exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ`).

## `regularLocal_inductive_step` (line 1008 / closure body L1094)

### Attempt 1
- **Approach**: build an `(R⧸(x))`-linear equivalence between
  `R ⧸ (x • (⊤ : Submodule R R))` (i.e. `QuotSMulTop x R`) and
  `R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}`, then transport `hreg_q` via

- **Result**: RESOLVED axiom-clean modulo the still-sorry substrate
  helper `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` (no NEW
  axiom introduced).
- **Key insight**: the submodule equality `x • ⊤ = Ideal.span {x}` in
  `Submodule R R` holds by `ext`-membership unfolding via
  `Submodule.mem_smul_pointwise_iff_exists` +
  `Ideal.mem_span_singleton` + `Dvd.dvd`. The `R⧸(x)`-linear equiv is
  built explicitly with `Submodule.mapQ _ _ LinearMap.id heq.le`
  forward and `Submodule.mapQ _ _ LinearMap.id heq.ge` backward;
  `map_smul'` reduces to `rfl` after `Quotient.inductionOn` on the
  scalar (the `R⧸(x)`-action on both modules is `[s] • [r] = [s * r]`
  definitionally — on `R⧸(x)` via the ring action on itself, on
  `QuotSMulTop x R` via the `Module.IsTorsionBy.module` mechanism
  installed by `instQuotientIdealSpanSingletonSetSubmoduleHSMulTop`).
- **Lemmas found / used**:
  - `Submodule.mem_smul_pointwise_iff_exists` (membership in element
    pointwise smul).
  - `Ideal.mem_span_singleton` (membership in principal ideal).
  - `Submodule.mapQ` (linear map between quotients).
  - `LinearEquiv.isRegular_congr` (transport `IsRegular` along
    linear equiv over same ring).
- **Net file diff**: +~25 LOC for the `heq` proof + the explicit
  `LinearEquiv` construction. The `private lemma`
  `regularLocal_inductive_step` is now **axiom-clean modulo**
  `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` (the consolidated
  Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gap at L975). `lean_verify` on
